title: Whether expat parents owe their children fluency in the local language
summary: Argue whether parents raising children abroad have a moral obligation to ensure those children grow up fluent in the host country's language.
topic: The duty of expat parents to teach their children the language of the country they live in
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether parents who raise children abroad — in places such as Tashkent, Hanoi, Istanbul, or Mexico City — have a moral obligation to ensure those children become genuinely fluent in the host country's language, or whether maintaining the family's first language is the higher priority. Use a cleft sentence, an inversion or concession, at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Reference at least one specific city, school system, or family pattern. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What expat children inherit from their parents' linguistic choices is far more consequential than most parents…
Admittedly, the practical case for prioritising the family's first language inside the home is not without…
Raised in Tashkent for eleven years but enrolled exclusively in an international school where Russian was barely heard, one young woman…

hintVocab.meaningVi: quốc gia nơi mình đang sống / nơi đang được đón nhận
di sản ngôn ngữ

hintVocab.phrase: to be raised in a bubble
hintVocab.meaningVi: được nuôi dạy trong một bong bóng (tách biệt với xã hội)
trường quốc tế
ngôn ngữ di sản (tiếng mẹ đẻ giữ trong gia đình)
người con của người sống xa quê / thế hệ con của expat
thông thạo ngôn ngữ địa phương

hintVocab.phrase: to integrate into a host society
hintVocab.meaningVi: hòa nhập vào xã hội nơi mình sống
đặc quyền về ngôn ngữ
chuyển đổi ngôn ngữ trong giao tiếp
bị từ chối quyền tiếp cận
sự thân thiết về văn hóa
ngôn ngữ của sân trường
một người ngoài cuộc lịch sự
lớn lên trong sự phiên dịch (luôn qua trung gian ngôn ngữ)
nhiệm kỳ ngoại giao / công tác nước ngoài
khoán việc hòa nhập cho người khác
sự nuôi dạy đơn ngữ
giao tiếp ngang hàng
nghĩa vụ ngôn ngữ
giai đoạn thuận lợi để học ngôn ngữ ở trẻ em

sampleText:
An expat child growing up in a city such as Tashkent or Hanoi will, by the time they are eighteen, have spent more of their life there than their parents ever will. They will have stood in its queues, ridden its buses, watched its television in the background of a thousand evenings. Whether they can speak to a taxi driver, an elderly neighbour, or the cleaner at their own school in that city's language is, in almost every case, a direct consequence of decisions their parents made about schooling, after-school time, and weekend friendships.

What expat children inherit from their parents' linguistic choices is far more consequential than most parents acknowledge at the time. A child enrolled exclusively in an English-medium international school, kept inside an expatriate social circle, and bussed home before extracurriculars begin will not pick up Uzbek or Vietnamese by osmosis. They will pick up enough phrases to order food and ask directions, and they will spend the next forty years calling that 'speaking the language'.

Admittedly, the case for protecting a family's first language inside the home is not without force. Children whose home language is allowed to weaken often lose the ability to talk meaningfully with grandparents, to read the books their parents grew up with, or to understand jokes that depend on cultural reference. Heritage-language maintenance is a real responsibility. Nobody is suggesting that families should switch to the local language at the dinner table.

Even so, the choice is rarely binary. What a thoughtful linguistic strategy looks like is a home in which the heritage language is spoken and read consistently, combined with a deliberate effort to give the child genuine, daily exposure to the local language outside it — through a local-stream school for at least part of the week, through neighbourhood friendships not curated by the parents, through after-school activities where the language of the schoolyard is the language of the country. None of this happens automatically. All of it requires that the parents themselves treat the host language as worth learning.

Granted, this is harder for short-term postings — a two-year diplomatic assignment, a three-year corporate rotation — than for long-term residents. But even short-term families can model respect for the local language without committing to fluency, and they can resist the easy comfort of an expatriate bubble that treats integration as someone else's problem.

What children deserve from their parents, in the end, is a chance to belong to the place they grew up in, not merely to have visited it for two decades. To deny them the local language is to make that belonging impossible.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to redefine the stakes for the child
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What expat children inherit from their parents' linguistic choices is far more consequential than most parents acknowledge at the time' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để chuyển trọng tâm từ hành vi của cha mẹ sang hậu quả thực tế đối với con cái. Thay vì viết 'Parents' linguistic choices have consequences for their children', câu chẻ đôi đẩy mệnh đề trừu tượng 'far more consequential than most parents acknowledge' vào vị trí nhấn mạnh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What expat children inherit from their parents' linguistic choices is far more consequential than most parents acknowledge at the time' uses a what-cleft to shift focus from parental behaviour to the child's actual consequences. Rather than 'Parents' linguistic choices have consequences for their children', the cleft pushes the abstract assessment 'far more consequential than most parents acknowledge' into the emphatic position.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Reduced participle clause stacking to compress a description
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A child enrolled exclusively in an English-medium international school, kept inside an expatriate social circle, and bussed home before extracurriculars begin will not pick up Uzbek or Vietnamese by osmosis' dùng ba mệnh đề phân từ rút gọn liên tiếp ('enrolled… kept… bussed…') để chồng chất nhiều đặc điểm vào một chủ ngữ duy nhất. Cách viết này nén ba quyết định riêng biệt của cha mẹ vào một câu duy nhất, và sự lặp lại nhịp điệu nhấn mạnh tính có hệ thống của các lựa chọn này.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A child enrolled exclusively in an English-medium international school, kept inside an expatriate social circle, and bussed home before extracurriculars begin will not pick up Uzbek or Vietnamese by osmosis' stacks three reduced past-participle clauses ('enrolled… kept… bussed…') to pile descriptive load onto a single subject. The structure compresses three separate parental decisions into one sentence, and the rhythmic repetition underlines how systematic these choices are.

sampleTranslationVi:
Một đứa trẻ con của người xa quê lớn lên trong một thành phố như Tashkent hay Hà Nội, đến năm mười tám tuổi, sẽ đã sống ở đó nhiều hơn cha mẹ chúng từng sống. Chúng sẽ đã đứng xếp hàng ở đó, đi xe buýt ở đó, xem truyền hình của nó trong nền của hàng nghìn buổi tối. Việc chúng có thể nói chuyện với một tài xế taxi, một người hàng xóm cao tuổi, hay người dọn dẹp ở chính trường của mình bằng ngôn ngữ của thành phố đó là, trong hầu hết mọi trường hợp, hậu quả trực tiếp của những quyết định mà cha mẹ chúng đã đưa ra về trường học, thời gian sau giờ học, và tình bạn cuối tuần.

Điều mà những đứa trẻ con của người xa quê thừa hưởng từ những lựa chọn ngôn ngữ của cha mẹ chúng có hậu quả lớn hơn nhiều so với những gì hầu hết cha mẹ thừa nhận vào thời điểm đó. Một đứa trẻ được ghi danh độc quyền tại một trường quốc tế giảng dạy bằng tiếng Anh, được giữ bên trong một vòng tròn xã hội của người nước ngoài, và được đưa về nhà trước khi các hoạt động ngoại khóa bắt đầu sẽ không tự học được tiếng Uzbek hay tiếng Việt qua thẩm thấu. Chúng sẽ học đủ cụm từ để gọi món ăn và hỏi đường, và chúng sẽ dành bốn mươi năm tiếp theo gọi đó là 'biết tiếng'.

Thừa nhận rằng, lập luận về việc bảo vệ ngôn ngữ thứ nhất của gia đình bên trong nhà không phải là không có sức nặng. Những đứa trẻ mà ngôn ngữ gia đình bị cho phép suy yếu thường mất đi khả năng nói chuyện có ý nghĩa với ông bà, đọc những cuốn sách mà cha mẹ chúng lớn lên cùng, hay hiểu được những câu đùa phụ thuộc vào tham chiếu văn hóa. Việc duy trì ngôn ngữ di sản là một trách nhiệm thực sự. Không ai gợi ý rằng các gia đình nên chuyển sang ngôn ngữ địa phương ở bàn ăn tối.

Dẫu vậy, sự lựa chọn hiếm khi là nhị phân. Một chiến lược ngôn ngữ chu đáo trông như thế nào là một ngôi nhà nơi ngôn ngữ di sản được nói và đọc một cách nhất quán, kết hợp với một nỗ lực có chủ ý để cho đứa trẻ tiếp xúc thực sự, hàng ngày với ngôn ngữ địa phương bên ngoài ngôi nhà — thông qua một trường học bằng tiếng địa phương ít nhất một phần của tuần, thông qua những tình bạn hàng xóm không được cha mẹ sắp xếp, thông qua các hoạt động ngoài giờ học nơi ngôn ngữ của sân trường là ngôn ngữ của đất nước. Không điều nào trong số này xảy ra một cách tự động. Tất cả đều đòi hỏi chính cha mẹ coi ngôn ngữ của nước sở tại là đáng để học.

Thừa nhận rằng, điều này khó khăn hơn đối với các nhiệm kỳ ngắn hạn — một nhiệm vụ ngoại giao hai năm, một vòng luân chuyển doanh nghiệp ba năm — so với các cư dân dài hạn. Nhưng ngay cả các gia đình ngắn hạn cũng có thể thể hiện sự tôn trọng đối với ngôn ngữ địa phương mà không cần cam kết đạt được sự thông thạo, và họ có thể kháng cự lại sự thoải mái dễ dàng của một bong bóng người nước ngoài coi việc hòa nhập là vấn đề của người khác.

Điều mà trẻ em xứng đáng nhận được từ cha mẹ, cuối cùng, là một cơ hội để thuộc về nơi chúng lớn lên, không chỉ đơn thuần là đã ghé thăm nó trong hai thập kỷ. Từ chối ngôn ngữ địa phương đối với chúng là làm cho việc thuộc về đó trở nên không thể.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a 'what' cleft to highlight what children inherit?
options: Children inherit what is more consequential from their parents most acknowledge.
What expat children inherit from their parents' linguistic choices is far more consequential than most parents acknowledge at the time.
Far more consequential what expat children inherit from linguistic choices is parents most acknowledge.
Acknowledge the linguistic choices what expat children inherit from is far more consequential parents.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' delays the most important claim ('far more consequential than most parents acknowledge') to the emphatic end position.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the new evaluation placed after 'is'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'heritage language' mean in this essay?
options: An ancient, no longer spoken language
The family's first language, often maintained at home in a country where another language dominates
A language officially recognised as part of a country's cultural heritage
A language children must take in school as part of the curriculum
mcQuestions.explanation: A heritage language is the language of a child's family of origin, typically maintained at home in an environment where the wider society speaks a different language.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'heritage' as 'family inheritance' — what kind of language would that be?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'admittedly' correctly as a concession?
options: Admittedly, the case for protecting a family's first language inside the home is not without force.
The case admittedly protecting first language without force is the home inside.
Inside the home admittedly without force is the case protecting first language.
Without force admittedly the case is protecting inside the home first language.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly,' followed by a complete clause concedes a real point to the opposing view before the essay continues to its main position.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Admittedly,' before a full, well-formed sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses stacked reduced participle clauses to compress description?
options: A child enrolled exclusively in an English-medium international school, kept inside an expatriate social circle, and bussed home before extracurriculars begin will not pick up Uzbek or Vietnamese by osmosis.
Enrolled kept bussed a child in expatriate circle international school extracurriculars Uzbek Vietnamese osmosis pick up will not.
Will not pick up by osmosis enrolled in international school kept inside expatriate circle a child bussed home Uzbek Vietnamese.
A child Uzbek Vietnamese will pick up enrolled in school kept in circle bussed home.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'enrolled… kept… bussed…' are three reduced past-participle clauses describing a single subject ('A child'), compressing three parental decisions efficiently.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for three -ed participle phrases all modifying the same noun before the main verb appears.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to pick something up by osmosis' mean?
options: To learn through formal lessons and textbooks
To absorb something gradually and unconsciously simply by being around it
To memorise something using repetition drills
To translate something carefully word by word
mcQuestions.explanation: Osmosis is the biological process by which substances pass through a membrane; metaphorically it means absorbing something — like a language — passively from one's environment.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of how a sponge absorbs water without any active effort.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly foregrounds what happens to the child's language?
options: Parents allow their children's home language to weaken.
Children whose home language is allowed to weaken often lose the ability to talk meaningfully with grandparents.
Weakening the home language children lose grandparents to talk meaningfully.
Grandparents whose home language is weakening allow children to lose talking meaningfully.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is allowed to weaken' is a passive construction that places the child's home language in subject position, foregrounding what is happening to it rather than who is permitting it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence makes 'home language' the subject of the passive verb 'is allowed'?

options: To hire a company to handle the legal paperwork for moving abroad
To assume that someone else will do the work of helping your family fit into the host society
To pay for language lessons taught by an outside agency
To employ a local nanny who speaks the host country's language
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Outsource' means hand off to someone outside; 'outsourcing integration' is the parental habit of assuming a school, a nanny, or some other party will do the work of integrating the family.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean to 'outsource' a task that is really your own responsibility?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'they will spend the next forty years calling that speaking the language'?
options: They will speak the language fluently for the next four decades.
They will spend forty years pretending to speak a language they barely know.
They will mistake a tourist's vocabulary for actual fluency for the rest of their adult lives.
They will refuse to call themselves speakers of the language for forty years.
mcQuestions.explanation: The sentence is ironic: the expat child has only ordering-food vocabulary but will overestimate this for the rest of their life, calling minimal competence 'speaking the language'.
mcQuestions.hint: What does the writer think the child actually knows, compared to what they will claim to know?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the choice is rarely binary.
On the contrary, the home language is unimportant.
Furthermore, the home language must always lose.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (heritage-language maintenance is real) and pivots to the main argument that this need not be a binary choice.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: Expat parents should abandon their first language entirely in favour of the local language.
Expat parents should send their children only to international schools.
Expat parents owe their children genuine exposure to the local language alongside heritage-language maintenance.
Expat children should not be raised abroad if their parents are unable to teach them the local language.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues for a thoughtful, both-and strategy: keep the heritage language strong at home AND build genuine local-language fluency outside it.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the fourth paragraph, which describes 'what a thoughtful linguistic strategy looks like'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly parents need to chill out and let kids learn what they want.
Expat kids are basically tourists in their own city if they don't pick up the language.
Children raised abroad without genuine exposure to the host language are placed at a structural disadvantage in their own daily environment.
It's just kind of weird if a kid grows up somewhere for ten years and can't talk to people there.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('genuine exposure', 'structural disadvantage', 'daily environment') and avoids contractions, intensifiers, and slang.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'basically', 'kind of', and other colloquial markers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'code-switching' mean in a linguistic context?
options: Programming a computer to translate text
Alternating between two or more languages within a conversation, often within a single sentence
Using secret codes to communicate with another speaker
Switching the keyboard layout on a phone
mcQuestions.explanation: Code-switching is the practice — common among bilingual speakers — of moving between languages depending on context, listener, or topic, sometimes within one utterance.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'code' as a language system, and 'switch' as moving between them.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause to define a particular kind of child?
options: Children whose home language is allowed to weaken often lose the ability to talk meaningfully with grandparents.
Whose home language children grandparents to talk meaningfully often lose allowed is to weaken.
Grandparents meaningfully to talk often lose the ability children whose home language is allowed to weaken.
Allowed to weaken whose home language grandparents talk meaningfully children often lose.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'whose home language is allowed to weaken' is a defining relative clause specifying which children the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun immediately and start with 'who', 'which', 'whose', or 'that'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'a polite outsider' suggest about an expat child's relationship to the host country?
options: They are formally respected guests of the government.
They have manners but no genuine connection to the place they grew up in.
They are tolerated but not allowed to speak in public.
They are diplomats with special legal status.
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase suggests social distance: someone who is unfailingly polite but never quite belongs — a status the essay argues children deserve to be spared from.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean to be 'polite but outside' — to be welcomed without ever truly being part?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay with a strong moral claim?
options: In conclusion, families should think about language carefully.
Therefore, all expat parents must send their children to local schools immediately.
To deny them the local language is to make that belonging impossible.
To sum up, expat life is complicated and there are no easy answers.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C is a sharp, specific moral claim that directly states what the essay believes is at stake — belonging — and what threatens it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option states a specific cost rather than a vague generalisation?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best summarises the essay's view of the 'expatriate bubble'?
options: A protective environment that helps children adjust to life abroad
A social and linguistic enclosure that prevents children from genuinely entering the host society
A diplomatic facility that requires special access
A school system widely admired for its rigour
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay treats the 'expatriate bubble' negatively — as a comfortable enclosure that allows families to avoid genuine integration and treats it as someone else's problem.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at how the bubble is described in the fifth paragraph.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If you were a parent raising a child in a country whose language you did not speak well, how would you actually structure your child's week to give them real fluency in the local language — without sacrificing your family's first language? What specifically would you change?
